Chemical Equivalence
Chemically equivalent groups show up as one signal on the NMR spectrum
Homotopic groups – equivalent and always indistinguishable
Enantiotopic groups – equivalent except in chiral environments
Diastereotopic groups – nonequivalent under all conditions
How many types of hydrogen atoms are found in:
methanol ethanol
Test: substitute, switch and determine stereochemical relationship

Determination of chemical equivalence:
If replacement of each hydrogen in turn by an imaginary group, Z,
generates the same compound or the enantiomer, the hydrogens are equivalent.
Chemically Equivalent Hydrogens

Chemically Equivalent Hydrogens
Determination of chemical equivalence:

Relative Chemical Shift:
–Chemical shifts are measured relative to an internal standard,
tetramethylsilane (TMS). By convention TMS is assigned a
chemical shift of zero
–H's are shielded with respect to the H's of most
organic compounds.
–12 equivalent hydrogens
–Inert toward most organic compounds
–Low boiling point (27°C)

Evidence of Spin-Spin Splitting

1 H NMR Spectrum of Trichloroethane
n + 1 rule: n adjacent protons cause the resonance to be split into n + 1 lines
Spin-spin splitting: protons affect the absorption of neighboring protons in a reciprocal fashion

First-Order Rules
1. N + 1  Rule: N equivalent protons split a signal into N + 1 peaks.
2. Signal Intensity given by Pascal's Triangle:

s – singlet
d – doublet
t – triplet
q – quartet
quin – quintet
sextet
septet
octet
…
m – multiplet
dd – doublet of doublets
dt – doublet of triplets

Coupling Constant, J xy
Coupling constants are independent of magnetic field strength.
Reciprocation of Coupling Constants: Two magnetically coupled
protons share the same coupling constants
Equivalent hydrogens do not signal split each other.

Jxy is typically 6 - 7 Hz for vicinal coupling.
Signal splitting is usually not observed for hydrogens separated
by more than three s -bonds.

1 H NMR Spectra of Equilibrium Processes
(A) Conformational Changes
Two types of hydrogens:
Multiplet @ 1.12 ppm
(6 axial H's)
1H NMR Spectrum @ -100ºC
interconverts axial and
equatorial hydrogens .
fast on the 1H NMR time
scale at room temperature.
Multiplet @ 1.60 ppm
(6 equatorial H's)
1H NMR Spectrum @ 25ºC
Singlet @ 1.36 ppm
(all 12 H's)
axial
equatorial

1 H NMR Spectra of Equilibrium Processes
(B) Proton Exchange
The hydroxy proton exchanges many time during the NMR experiment.
(1) The differing chemical environments of the exchanging hydrogen
often leads to broaden peaks.
(2) No signal splitting between the exchanging hydrogen and neighboring hydrogens due to averaging of all possible spin arrangements.
Two consequences:
HO–CH 2 –CH 3

(also –NH protons)
–OH and –NH protons can be “washed out” with a D 2 O shake
Visible in NMR
Invisible in NMR
The “D 2 O shake”
-a way to simplify spectra complicated by –OH and –NH signals

e.g.:  C 3 H 6
(a) Saturated (has maximum number of hydrogens ): C n H 2n + 2
e.g.:  CH 3 CH 2 CH 3
where n is the number of carbons
DBE =
(2n + 2) – H count
2

(2 x 3 + 2) – 6
2

Possibilities:

(II) Compounds with Heteroatoms:
saturated                     saturated
6 hydrogens                5 hydrogens
Ignore divalent atoms in the hydrogen count
(a) Monovalent atoms (e.g., halides):
Count a monovalent atom as a hydrogen
(b) Divalent atoms (e.g., oxygen):
saturated                       saturated
6 hydrogens                  6 hydrogens
Double Bond Equivalents/Degree of Unsaturation

(c) Trivalent atoms (e.g., nitrogen):
Subtract one hydrogen from the hydrogen count for each trivalent atom present.
saturated saturated
6 hydrogens                  7 hydrogens
Examples of Unsaturated Compounds:
C 3 H 6 O C 6 H 6
(II) Compounds with Heteroatoms:
Double Bond Equivalents/Degree of Unsaturation

Interpretation Guidelines for 1 H NMR Spectra
Determine the double bond equivalents from the molecular formula.
Tabulate chemical shifts and the number of hydrogens from integration.
Check hydrogen count from the 1 H NMR spectrum versus molecular formula.
Look for characteristic shifts to determine the functional groups present (i.e. carboxylic acids, phenyls, alkenyls, alkynyls, carbons bearing electronegative atoms).
Guess at possible groups based on chemical shifts and hydrogen count.
Keep in mind:
-An integral value of three is typically due to a methyl group.
-An integral value of two is typically due to a methylene group.
-An integral value of four is typically due to two methylene groups.
-A methylene group in the 2.8 – 3.5 ppm range is typically due to the attachment of the methylene carbon to a heteroatom.

Keep in mind (continued): -A methylene group in the 1.7 – 2.7 ppm range is typically due to the attachment of the methylene carbon to a sp 2 carbon.
-Benzene hydrogens come in the 7.0 – 9.0 ppm range. Integration gives the number of hydrogens around the ring.
-A broad singlet is typically due to a hydroxy hydrogen.
Use splitting patterns to put the possible groups together :
-First order rules.
-An ethyl group is characterized by the presence of a triplet (methyl group) and a quartet (methylene group).
-Chemically shift equivalent hydrogens do not spin-spin split.
-Lack of splitting often indicates symmetry.
Interpretation Guidelines for 1 H NMR Spectra
